title: Listening - Part 4: Living Without Social Media
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
topic: five people discussing their decision to delete all their social media accounts

content.audio_script: Speaker 1: I work as a graphic designer, and [21]I felt I was constantly comparing my progress to the curated highlights of everyone else in my industry[/21]. It was toxic for my self-esteem. Since deleting my accounts, [26]the most significant change has been the massive boost in my creative originality[/26]. Without the constant noise of other people's work, I’ve finally started to develop my own unique style again. It’s like I’ve cleared a mental fog that I didn't even know was there. || Speaker 2: My main motivation was privacy. [22]I became increasingly uncomfortable with how much personal data these platforms were collecting and selling[/22]. It felt like a violation. I thought I’d feel isolated, but [27]I was surprised by how much more meaningful my face-to-face interactions have become[/27]. When I meet friends now, we actually have things to talk about because I haven't already seen their dinner on my feed. We listen more intently to each other. || Speaker 3: I’m a student, and [23]I realized I was wasting three to four hours a day mindlessly scrolling, which was seriously affecting my grades[/23]. I needed my time back. The first week was hard, but [28]the most challenging part has been the feeling of being 'out of the loop' regarding local events and parties[/28]. I’ve missed several birthdays simply because the invites were only on Facebook. I’ve had to ask my close friends to text me directly, which makes me feel a bit demanding. || Speaker 4: I’m a journalist, and [24]I found the constant stream of negative news and toxic comments was severely affecting my mental health[/24]. I was always in a state of high alert. Deleting social media was a survival tactic. [29]The most rewarding outcome has been the rediscovery of my ability to focus on long-form books[/29]. My attention span had been ruined by bite-sized posts, but now I can spend hours reading without checking my phone. My brain finally feels like it has room to breathe again. || Speaker 5: I’d used social media for my small business for years, but [25]I decided to quit because I was tired of the constant pressure to be 'on' and perform for an audience[/25]. It was exhausting. I’ve moved to a newsletter instead. [30]What I value most now is the sense of complete control over my daily schedule[/30]. I no longer wake up and immediately respond to comments or likes. My mornings are mine again, and that sense of autonomy has made me much more productive and less stressed in my work life.

task1.title: Task One
task1.instruction: For questions 21–25, choose from the list (A–H) each speaker's main reason for quitting social media.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 mentions comparing himself to others' curated highlights and the impact on self-esteem.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 was uncomfortable with how personal data was being collected and sold.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 mentions wasting hours scrolling and the negative impact on his studies.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 found the negative news and toxic comments were affecting her mental health.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 was tired of the pressure to perform for an audience and always be 'on'.

options.A: to avoid a specific online conflict
options.E: to stop comparing themselves to others
options.G: to reduce the pressure of self-promotion

task2.title: Task Two
task2.instruction: For questions 26–30, choose from the list (A–H) what each speaker mentions as an outcome of their decision.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 highlights the massive boost in his creative originality.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 mentions that her face-to-face interactions have become more meaningful.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 finds it challenging to be 'out of the loop' for social events.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 notes the rediscovery of her ability to focus on long-form reading.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 values the sense of control over his time and daily schedule.

options.A: the difficulty of staying socially informed
options.B: a significant increase in physical activity
options.C: the loss of certain business opportunities
options.D: a renewed sense of artistic independence
options.E: the pleasure of reclaiming their time
options.F: a restored capacity for deep concentration
options.G: the discovery of a new offline hobby
options.H: an improvement in the quality of conversation
